item.title: My AI prompts for code review
item.description: Here is my small collection of AI prompts.
item.content:encoded:
Here is my small collection of AI prompts. Most probably I should change them into the SKILLs or something like that, but as I share them across many other agentic tools it is not such easy.

Prompts in this post:

  • What is important for me in AI review prompts
    • One comment per file
    • Assess your work!
  • [PROMPT] You HATE this implementation
    • Prompt

What is important for me in AI review prompts​

One comment per file​

I've noticed that, nevertheless of the tool I use (Copilot CLI, Gemini CLI, Jules, droid) having review in a terminal is really cumbersome. The wall of text is unreadable and unmanageable. That's why it is beneficial to instruct an agent to:

  1. Create every review comment in a separate file.
  2. Open those files in code - this trick saves a ton of time. Especially that I close file in IDE, as I am done with them or moving to folder like "closed" or even deleting to forget. Everything to keep the environment clean.

This allows me to carefully review file by file, issue by issue, and not being overwhelmed by the amount. I can also put comments in the file and ask an agent to review my comments and redo its review (sic!) based on my input, and then open the file in code again of course!

Assess your work!​

Another important thing is to ask an Agent for assessment of it's own work. This is quite common technique, which you can find across many prompts. I've noticed that this significantly increases the quality of the results. I also love adding things like "Add 3 argument pro your proposals and against" to some of the prompts if I want the more detailed insight.

[PROMPT] You HATE this implementation​

Base for this one is taken from here: https://www.reddit.com/r/ClaudeAI/comments/1q5a90l/so_i_stumbled_across_this_prompt_hack_a_couple/. I found it when I hadn't access to my own prompts and I needed something to run with Google's Jules. As Jules reject writing normal review comments on GitHub I have developed something like that.

So this is oriented to work well in web only interface of Google's Jules - notice the hard break of lines, what instruct this tool to also keep lines short. Of course, you can instruct Jules to send this review as a pull request, but I never found it comfortable.

Notice the last sentence, which tells to propose split of the PR. My PRs too often contains too many changes, as I really like to improve the code base as I am reading it.

This is the first prompt I've met which takes "HATE" approach. In comparison to the approach "review DRY, SOLID..." there is a visible difference. It is hard to say for me at this moment how to call it, but it seems to be a good addition to more structured approach.

So the main sentence here is "and you HATE this implementation", which sets the tone and, surprisingly, approach. It must be interesting training data, that the hateful reviewers has something valuable to say :D.

Prompt​

Do a git diff and pretend you're a senior dev doing a code review from current branch against the `release` branch, and you HATE this implementation. What would you criticize? What edge cases am I missing? 
 
Every comment write to separate {number}-{slug}.md file. under /.review/ folder. Number is just a next unique number, like a local id.
 
Every file should have a structure like a proper review comment on github: 
- Location - put the problem context, where to find an issue you are criticizing. Say what this/those lines of code were supposed to do.
  Put at least one line before and one line after. You can add more lines if it is needed for context.
- Say what is wrong and why in a professional way. 
- Propose a solution. If appropriate provide a code block. But only for simple changes.
- Do not touch actual code. you can only create those md files under /.review/ folder.
- Assess importance of the comment review in scale from 1 to 6, when 6 is an excellent, easy to implement and very valuable improvement and 1 is a poor idea, which you are unsure.
- Keep lines short, to keep them readable in narrow window.

## Example of expected output
Please format your response exactly like the example below for every issue you find.

Filename:
/.review/01-inefficient-memory-filtering.md
````md
    # Location and context
    `Controllers/OrderController.cs:29-35`
    
    This block is supposed to retrieve a user's orders asynchronously, calculate the 
    total amount, and return it as an API response.
    
    ```csharp
    28:  // Get user orders and calculate total
    29:  [HttpGet("{userId}/summary")]
    30:  public IActionResult GetOrderSummary(int userId)
    31:  {
    32:      var orders = _orderService.GetOrdersAsync(userId).Result;
    33:      var total = orders.Sum(o => o.Amount);
    34:      return Ok(new { UserId = userId, Total = total });
    35:  }
    36:
    ```
    
    # What is wrong
    Using `.Result` (or `.Wait()`) on an asynchronous Task inside a synchronous method 
    is a fatal architectural flaw known as "sync-over-async". By blocking the current 
    thread while waiting for the Task to complete, you are holding a thread hostage. 
    In an ASP.NET Core environment, doing this prevents the thread from returning to 
    the Thread Pool to serve other requests.

    The critical edge case you are missing here is application behavior under load. 
    While this works fine on your local machine with 1 user, during peak traffic, 
    a burst of concurrent requests will instantly exhaust the Thread Pool. This will 
    cause a deadlock or thread pool starvation, bringing the entire application to 
    a grinding halt (503 Service Unavailable). This implementation is actively dangerous 
    to system stability.
  
    # Proposed solution
  
    Embrace "async all the way down". Change the controller action to be asynchronous 
    and use the await keyword so the thread is yielded back to the pool during the 
    I/O operation.
  
    ```csharp
    [HttpGet("{userId}/summary")]
    public async Task<IActionResult> GetOrderSummary(int userId)
    {
        var orders = await _orderService.GetOrdersAsync(userId);
        var total = orders.Sum(o => o.Amount);
        return Ok(new { UserId = userId, Total = total });
    }
    ```

    # Assessment
    6/6 - it is easy to do and makes a code clean.
````

If you think that, the PR is doing way too much, propose in separate file how to split it into smaller ones.
Order them from smallest to biggest.

item.title: Welcome
item.description: Docusaurus blogging features are powered by the blog plugin.
item.content:encoded:
Docusaurus blogging features are powered by the blog plugin.

Here are a few tips you might find useful.

Simply add Markdown files (or folders) to the blog directory.

Regular blog authors can be added to authors.yml.

The blog post date can be extracted from filenames, such as:

  • 2019-05-30-welcome.md
  • 2019-05-30-welcome/index.md

A blog post folder can be convenient to co-locate blog post images:

The blog supports tags as well!

And if you don't want a blog: just delete this directory, and use blog: false in your Docusaurus config.

item.title: MDX Blog Post
item.description: Blog posts support Docusaurus Markdown features, such as MDX.
item.content:encoded:
Blog posts support Docusaurus Markdown features, such as MDX.

tip

Use the power of React to create interactive blog posts.

For example, use JSX to create an interactive button:

<button onClick={() => alert('button clicked!')}>Click me!</button>

item.title: Long Blog Post
item.description: This is the summary of a very long blog post,
item.content:encoded:
This is the summary of a very long blog post,

Use a {/* truncate */} comment to limit blog post size in the list view.

Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit. Pellentesque elementum dignissim ultricies. Fusce rhoncus ipsum tempor eros aliquam consequat. Lorem ipsum dolor sit amet

item.title: First Blog Post
item.content:encoded:
